text: The surgeon uses the non-dominant hand to medialize the medial umbilical ligament, then incises the peritoneum at the intersection of the ligament and the vas deferens.
The surgeon performs dissection to expose the left endopelvic fascia, then repeats the procedure on the right side to achieve symmetrical exposure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the retropubic space, fully exposing the pubic bone.
The surgeon preserves the median umbilical ligament to prevent excessive mobilization of the bladder, maintaining the anatomical integrity of the structure.
The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia with cold scissors, starting laterally to the tendinous arch on the left side and then proceeding to the right side.
The surgeon places a stitch on the anterior surface of the prostate, positioned caudally to the bladder neck limit.
The surgeon uses a large needle driver to lift the prostate by pulling on the stitch placed on its anterior surface, demonstrating the manipulation of the prostate tissue.
The surgeon visualizes the bladder, appearing as an inverted V shape, positioned behind the base of the prostate.
The surgeon performs the dissection of the bladder neck using a posterolateral approach, carefully separating the tissue in this region.
The surgeon begins to identify the posterior vesicoprostatic angle, focusing on the anatomical junction between the bladder and prostate.
The surgeon uses a bipolar grasper to protect the detrusor muscle while extending the dissection to the midline with monopolar shears.
The surgeon uses the non-dominant hand to apply sufficient tension, exposing the cleavage plane and revealing the longitudinal detrusor fibers.
The surgeon identifies the longitudinal fibers of the urethra along the midline and carefully spares them during the dissection.
The surgeon opens the urethra and lifts the prostate by applying traction on the catheter, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon incises the posterior wall of the urethra while applying traction, facilitating the continuation of the posterior dissection of the bladder neck.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the vas deferens, placing a Hem-o-lok clip on the vas and its accompanying artery before cutting them.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm to lift the vas deferens while completing the dissection of the seminal vesicle with a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques.
Metallic clips can be helpful to minimize the use of energy.
The surgeon develops the posterior plane while carefully preserving the Denonvilliers' fascia, maintaining the integrity of this anatomical structure.
The surgeon continues blunt dissection down to the apex and extends laterally, approaching the neurovascular bundle.
The surgeon detaches and dissects the veil of Aphrodite, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs an anterior release of the neurovascular bundle, visibly maximizing the preservation of the bundle during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to avoid stretching the neurovascular bundle during the procedure.
The neurovascular bundle is observed falling down and laterally towards the vascular pedicle, following the completion of dissection.
The surgeon uses the non-dominant hand to lift the prostate, forming a ninety-degree angle with the vascular prostatic pedicle, which is then safely clipped and resected.
The surgeon performs the same maneuver on the right side, progressing until reaching the deep venous complex (DVC).
It's important not to stretch the bundle and to obtain a thick aflat veil in order to optimize the probability of functional recovery.
The surgeon dissects and cuts the dorsal venous complex (DVC) and the pulvoprostatic ligament, carefully preserving the surrounding periurethral tissue.
The surgeon closes the dorsal venous complex (DVC) using a running suture technique, securing the area with precise stitching.

The surgeon performs apical dissection at the prostatic apex using the collar technique, carefully following the anatomical contours to maximize urethral length and minimize the risk of positive surgical margins.
The surgeon performs posterior reconstruction using a 30 cm V-lock suture, carefully aligning and securing the tissue layers.
The surgeon places three stitches from left to right to approximate the remnants of the delombillae, vesicoprostatic muscle, and posterior urethral rhabdosphincter.
The surgeon continues the running suture from right to left using a thirty centimeter V-lock suture, incorporating the bladder neck and urethra in the second layer of three stitches.
The surgeon includes the mucosa in the suturing process to ensure alignment of the mucosal layers on both sides of the anastomosis.
The surgeon constructs the second layer of the anastomosis, forming the posterior plate by suturing the aligned mucosa on both sides.
The surgeon continues the anastomosis using a 23-centimeter running suture, starting from the posterior plate and proceeding in a counterclockwise direction.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis as the two running sutures converge at the midline, ensuring alignment and closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a leakage test by injecting 180 milliliters of saline solution into the anastomosis site to check for any leaks.

Between February and October, twenty-two, sixty-two patients affected by low or intermediate risk prostate cancer underwent bilateral nerve sparing RARP according to our technique using the HUGO robotic system.
All the procedures were completed without any intraoperative complications.
Median operative time was one hundred and fifty minutes while median consult time was one hundred and twenty minutes.
On average, the urethral catheter was removed on post-operative day two and the median length of stay was three days.
On final pathology, ten patients had the PT-tree disease while only three patients had positive surgical margins.
One month after surgery, fifty-nine percent of the patients recovered the continence while after three months, eighty-two percent were continence.
In conclusion, we were able to perform our RAR technique using the novel Hugera system, achieving optimal outcomes.
